title: Untitled (Twin Prongs)
description: Two elongated tapered prongs rise from a curved steel base, their sweeping lines suggesting both organic growth and industrial precision. The forged steel is characteristic of Feldman's work from the 1980s onward, when she turned from fiber and paper to metal as her primary material.

title: Untitled (Vessel)
description: A round bronze vessel form with serrated edges and a pointed finial, its patinated surface catching warm light through a central vertical opening. Bronze was one of Feldman's less common materials — she favored forged steel — and the vessel form with its serrated crown carries echoes of both her Metamorphosis-era organic shapes and the containment theme that ran through her entire career.

title: Untitled (Glass and Claw)
description: A blown glass orb is cradled by angular steel claws on a dark base, the transparency of glass in tension with the weight of forged metal. The piece exemplifies Feldman's signature late technique of combining blown glass with forged steel — vulnerability literally held by force.

title: Untitled (Diamond Plate)
description: A triangular floor sculpture built from industrial diamond plate steel with serrated edges and an iron wheel, evoking machinery and forward motion. The scavenged industrial materials connect this work to Feldman's War Toys series, which deployed found machine parts to critique the military-industrial complex.

title: Untitled (Cable Burst)
description: Thick braided steel cables cascade from a dark disc, their twisted strands frozen in mid-flow like a mechanical cascade. The braided cable links this work to Feldman's transition from fiber and textile materials in the 1970s to industrial metal — the cable retains the flexibility and tension of woven fiber while carrying industrial weight.

title: Untitled (Lattice Tower)
description: A tall, narrow lattice structure in welded steel, its ladder-like framework tapering to a waist before expanding again — an architectural form in miniature. The vertical steel lattice shares the vocabulary of Feldman's large-scale works like Jacob's Ladder (2011) and reflects her decades of architectural commission experience.

title: Untitled (Lantern I)
description: A wall-mounted work where a clear blown glass form is held within a steel armature, evoking an ancient lantern reimagined in sculptural terms. Part of the lineage of Feldman's Flasks of Fiction series (1998–2001), inspired by mosque lanterns she encountered while traveling in Turkey.

title: Untitled (Lantern II)
description: A dark, rounded glass form wrapped in a band of woven wire, suspended from a steel handle — mysterious and contained. The woven wire binding recalls Feldman's earlier fiber work while the glass-in-metal construction belongs to her Flasks of Fiction language.

title: Untitled (Lantern III)
medium: Forged steel, wire mesh, and frosted glass
description: Two interlocking steel rings frame a frosted glass element wrapped in wire mesh, creating a sculptural object of elegant geometric balance. The frosted glass softens the industrial armature — a variation on the containment-and-light theme Feldman explored across her Flasks of Fiction and later lantern works.

title: Wall Triptych (Installation View)
medium: Forged steel, blown glass, wire mesh
description: Three wall-mounted lantern forms displayed together, each combining steel and glass in a distinct variation on the theme of containment and light. Seen as a group, they demonstrate the range Feldman achieved within her Flasks of Fiction vocabulary — each piece resolves the tension between glass and metal differently.
